docs.title: Getting started
docs.text:
StormWeaver is a concurrent database testing tool, inspired by PStress.

It has two goals:

  • Provide a framework for writing highly concurrent somewhat randomized tests, to stress the database server, and to try to uncover syncrhonization/locking/memory management/... issues
  • Provide a framework for writing complex test scenarios in a user-friendly format (TODO)

Currently it works with PostgreSQL, and used for testing pg_tde.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
For a simple start, execute:

TODO

This will:

  1. Clone the StormWeaver repository in a folder called stormweaver
  2. Try to download the prebuilt binaries from github, if available, or report a failure.

Currently prebuilt binaries exists for Ubuntu 22.04 and Ubuntu 24.04.

Or alternatively it is possible to build StormWeaver from sources manually, for that see Building from source.

docs.title: A first scenario
docs.text:
StormWeaver stores test scenarios in the scenarios folder, written in lua. There is a scenario called example with lots of comments intended as a first example. To run it, execute the following command:

cd stormweaver
bin/stormweaver scenarios/example.lua [-c config/stormweaver.toml] [-i /path/to/the/pg/folder/] [scenario specific arguments...]

This script will:

  1. Setup a new data directory in datadirs/datadir_pr/...
  2. Start up PostgreSQL with this data directory
  3. Unless the WITHOUT_TDE environment varialbe is defined, it configures pg_tde
  4. Starts up the server instance
  5. Creates normal or encrypted tables based on the above setting
  6. Runs a 20 second workload on the server repeating, repeating 10 times. Each time it randomly restarts the server, or sends a kill -9 signal.

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
More details about the use of StormWeaver can be found in the following sections:

  1. About a quick high level overview of how the project is constructed from a users perspective
  2. About the configuration options supported by the default scripting framework
  3. About what functions are avaialble in the scenario scripts

docs.title: Getting the sources
docs.text:
StormWeaver uses git submodules. These are not included in the tarballs generated by github, to get the sources, use git clone --recursive, or git clone and initialize the submodules in a separate command:

git clone --recursive https://github.com/Percona-Lab/stormweaver.git

docs.title: Installing dependencies
docs.text:
The build process only requires two dependencies (other than git):

  • A modern C++ compilar, such as gcc and clang.
  • Conan 2, to download and build 3rd party libraries

The build process also uses CMake and ninja, but these will be installed by conan if not present on the system.

docs.title: Conan profile
docs.text:
After installing Conan 2 (using the instructions at https://docs.conan.io/2/installation.html), it also requires a profile setup.

A default profile can be created using the following command:

conan profile detect

The default profile usually selects an older C++ standard, while StormWeaver uses C++23. This can be changed by editing ~/.conan2/profiles/default and setting compiler.cppstd to 23.

docs.title: Building
docs.text:
The following command builds both StormWeaver and all of its conan managed dependencies:

conan build . --build=missing --settings=build_type=<Debug/Release>

During development, when the dependencies are already installed and only the main sources have to be rebuilt. the following commands provide a quicker result:

# or conan-release
cmake --build --target install --preset conan-debug

or with older CMake versions, which do not support this option:

# or Release
cd build/Debug
ninja install

docs.title: Advanced build options
docs.text:
StormWeaver can be built with various sanitizers for debugging purposes. These can be enabled in the conan build command with various options, for example to build with the address (asan) and undefined behavior (ubsan) sanitizers:

conan build . --build=missing --settings=build_type=<Debug/Release> -o '&:asan=True' -o '&:ubsan=True'

Similarly, msan and tsan are also supported.

Note

Not all sanitizers can be combined together in a single build. While the above example with asan and ubsan works, other combinations can result in either a build failure or false positives in the report.

docs.title: Lua C++ API Reference
docs.text:
This is a reference of the Lua API implemented directly in C++.

Note

Lua bindings are defined in libstormweaver/src/scripting/luactx.cpp

docs.title: sleep
docs.text:
sleep(milliseconds)

Sleeps for the specified time

docs.title: defaultActionRegistry
docs.text:
a = defaultActionRegistry()

Returns a modifiable reference to the default action registry

docs.title: initPostgresDatadir
docs.text:
Creates a new postgres data directory

pg = initPostgresDatadir('pg/intsall/dir', 'new/data/dir')

docs.title: initBasebackupFrom
docs.text:
Creates a new postgres data directory as a backup of a running postgres instance.

replica = initBasebackupFrom('pg/install/dir', 'new/data/dir', primaryNode) -- allows any number of additional parameters, added to the basebackup command line

docs.title: debug
docs.text: Writes a debug message to the log

docs.title: info
docs.text: Writes an info message to the log

docs.title: warning
docs.text: Writes a warning message to the log

docs.title: getenv
docs.text:
Returns a the value of an environment variable, or a default.

e = getenv("NAME", "default")

docs.title: setup_node_pg
docs.text:
Configures the connection parameters to a postgres installation.

return setup_node_pg({
  host = "localhost",
  port = 5432,
  user = "username",
  password = "",
  database = "stormweaver",
  on_connect = conn_callback,
})

docs.title: ActionFactory
docs.text: Used to create a specific action, stored in an ActionRegistry

docs.title: weight
docs.text:
A property representing the chance/weight of an action

action.weight = action.weight + 13;

docs.title: ActionRegistry
docs.text: Represents a set of actions executed by worker(s).

docs.title: remove
docs.text:
Removes the action with the name from the registry.

ar:remove("alter_table")

docs.title: insert
docs.text:
Inserts the specified ActionFactory to the registry. This function requires a specific Factory, which can be retrieved from another Registry using the get function.

ar1:insert(ar2:get("truncate"))

docs.title: has
docs.text:
Tells if the registry has a factory with the specified name.

if ar:has("alter_table) then
  --- ...
end

docs.title: makeCustomSqlAction
docs.text:
Creates a new custom SQL action with a specific query and weight.

ar:makeCustomSqlAction("checkpoint", "CHECKPOINT;", 1)

docs.title: makeCustomTableSqlAction
docs.text:
Creates a new custom SQL action related to a table, with a specific weight. The {table} expression in the SQL command is replaced with a randomly choosen table. Can be specified multiple times, and the same table will be used each time.

ar:makeCustomTableSqlAction("truncate_table", "TRUNCATE {table};", 2)

docs.title: get
docs.text:
Returns a reference to the factory with the specified name.

ar1:insert(ar2:get("truncate"))

docs.title: use
docs.text:
Overwrites the contents of the registry using the specified other registry. The two registries will be equal after executing this function.

ar1::use(ar2)

docs.title: fs
docs.text: A collection of filesystem operations.

docs.title: is_directory
docs.text:
Returns true if a directory with the specified name exists

if fs.is_directory('foo/bar') then
  -- ...
end

docs.title: copy_directory
docs.text:
Copies the directory recursively

fs.copy_directory('from', 'to')

docs.title: create_directory
docs.text:
Creates a new directory recursively

fs.create_directory('path/to/the/new/dir')

docs.title: delete_directory
docs.text:
Deletes the specified directory

fs.delete_directory('foo/bar')

docs.title: LoggedSQL
docs.text: A class representing a database connection

docs.title: execute_query
docs.text:
conn.execute_query("CREATE EXTENSION pg_tde;")

A simple funtion that execute a single SQL query. Result sets are not yet implemented in lua.

docs.title: Node
docs.text: A class representing a database server, created using the setupPgNode global function.

docs.title: init
docs.text:
Intended for node initialization, takes a callback that receives as a Worker as the single parameter.

function setup_fun(worker)
    worker:create_random_tables(5)
end
pgm.primaryNode:init(setup_fun)

docs.title: initRandomWorkload
docs.text:
Creates a new random workload setup with the given parameters.

Currently supports the following parameters:

  • run_seconds: how long to run the workload
  • worker_count: how many workers to use in the workload
node:initRandomWorkload({ run_seconds = 10, worker_count = 5 })

docs.title: possibleActions
docs.text:
Returns a reference to the action registry associated with the node. This is a copy made at the time of creating the node, and can be modified separately. Will be copied into the workload when one is created using initRandomWorkload.

a = node:possibleActions()

docs.title: Postgres
docs.text:
A class used to manage a Postgres installation/datadir.

An instance of this class can be created with the global functions initPostgresDatadir and initBasebackupFrom.

docs.title: start
docs.text:
Starts the server, returns true if succeeds.

pg1:start()

docs.title: stop
docs.text:
Stops the server Has a parameter, the graceful wait period - after that, it executes kill9.

pg1:stop(10)

docs.title: restart
docs.text:
Restarts (stops and starts) the server, returns true if succeeds. Has a parameter, the graceful wait period - after that, it executes kill9.

pg1:restart(10)

docs.title: kill9
docs.text:
Kills the server without waiting for it to stop.

pg1:kill9()

docs.title: createdb
docs.text:
Creates a database with the specified name.

pg1:createdb("foo")

docs.title: dropdb
docs.text:
Drops the database with the specified name.

pg1:dropdb("foo")

docs.title: createuser
docs.text:
Creates a user.

The first parameter is the name of the user, and the second parameter is an array of strings that gets added to the postgres createuser command.

pg1:createuser("stormweaver", {"-s"}

docs.title: is_running
docs.text:
Returns true if the server is running.

if pg1:is_running() then
 -- ...
end

docs.title: serverPort
docs.text:
Returns the port the server is using

p = pg1:serverPort()

docs.title: is_ready
docs.text:
Returns true if the server is ready to accept connections.

if pg1:is_ready then
  -- ...
end

docs.title: wait_ready
docs.text:
Waits up to the specified time in seconds, or until the server is ready.

if not pg1:wait_ready(20) then
  -- ...
end

docs.title: add_config
docs.text:
Appends the specified settings to the postgres configuration.

As Postgres only uses the last entry if the same setting is specified multiple times, this can also be used to modify existing settings.

pg1:add_config({
  logging_collector = "on",
  log_directory = "'logs'",
  log_filename = "'server.log'",
  log_min_messages = "'info'",
})

docs.title: add_hba
docs.text:
Adds an entry to the pg_hba.conf.

pg1:add_hba("host", "replication", "repuser", "127.0.0.1/32", "trust")

docs.title: BackgroundThread
docs.text:
Allows scripts to run something in the background.

For a more detailed example, see the section in the Lua Examples page.

docs.title: run
docs.text: Starts a background thread, using the specified logfile name and lua function.

docs.title: join
docs.text: Waits until the background thread completes.

docs.title: send
docs.text: Sends the specified string to the background thread.

docs.title: receive
docs.text: Receives a single string from the background thread - blocks and waits if it didn't send anything yet.

docs.title: receiveIfAny
docs.text: Receives a single string from the background thread, if it sent anything.

docs.title: RandomWorker
docs.text: Represents a randomized worker. It supports the same functions as the Worker, with the following additions:

docs.title: possibleActions
docs.text:
Returns a reference to the action registry associated with the specfic worker. This can be used to modify the actions executed by a single Worker, without affecting other Workers.

workload:worker(3):possibleActions():remove("alter_table")

docs.title: Worker
docs.text: A (non randomized) worker that can be used for initialization and other database related things.

docs.title: create_random_tables
docs.text:
Creates a specified number of tables according to the DDL configuration.

Currently the DDL configuration can't be modified.

worker:create_random_tables(5)

docs.title: sql_connection
docs.text:
Returns the SQL connection (LoggedSQL) of the worker, which can be used to execute SQL statements directly.

sql = worker:sql_connection()

docs.title: Workload
docs.text: Represents a testrun, it is created by Node using initRandomWorkload.

docs.title: run
docs.text:
Starts the workload.

The workload is executed in the background, this functions returns immediately after starting. This allows the script to modify the workload, or interact with the SQL servers while the workload is running.

workload:run()

docs.title: wait_completion
docs.text:
Waits until the currently running workload is completed.

workload:wait_completin()

docs.title: worker
docs.text:
Returns the Worker with the specified index.

Indexing starts with 1.

workload:worker(3)

docs.title: worker_count
docs.text:
Returns the number of workers.

workload:worker_count()

docs.title: reconnect_workers
docs.text: Reconnects any workers that lots their connection because of a restart.

docs.title: Randomized testing concepts
docs.text:
A main goal of StormWeaver is to allow scripted randomized testing.

This feature uses the following parts:

docs.title: Workload
docs.text:
A workload is a randomized test execution.

It is executed with some timelimit, using some number of Workers.

docs.title: Worker
docs.text:
A Worker is a thread executing Actions.

A randomized thread usually runs one or more workers, and each worker repeatedly chooses a random action and executes it, until the time limit is reached.

docs.title: Action
docs.text:
An Action is something executed during the test, usually in the form of one or more SQL statements.

For example:

  • Creating a table
  • Dropping a table
  • Altering a table
  • Inserting some data
  • ....

While there are some actions implemented in the C++ framework, it also makes it possible to define custom actions using an ActionRegistry

docs.title: ActionRegistry
docs.text:
An ActionRegistry is a collection of possible actions, and a definition of how these actions can be constructed. Other than the action definition itself, each Action in a Registry also has a weight, which determines the chance of its execution.

For each random action selection, the sum of all weights is calculated, and then a random number is choosen in the (0, sumOfWeights) range. Then the action is determined on where this number lands in the list of actions.

docs.title: Scripting architecture
docs.text:
StormWeaver relies on scripts written in Lua for test scenarios and utility code for the scenarios.

The C++ code is just a framework, it provides a helper architecture and executes a main lua script. If that script does nothing, the program just exits after startup.

There are also helper functions/classes written in lua, using the C++ code. The goal of these is to simplify the development of test scenarios, while also allowing easy modification without rebuilding the main executable.

docs.title: Folder structure
docs.text:
Lua scripts are stored in two directories:

  • scenarios is intended for specific test scenarios
  • scripts is intended for helper functions/classes usable by multiple scenarios

These directories are added to the search path at two locations:

  • Relative to the executable (which is bin/stormweaver, the directories have to be in the same folder as bin)
  • Relative to the current working directory

docs.title: 3rd party libraries
docs.text:
Additional, several 3rd party lua libraries are also included and are usable by scripts/scenarios. These are the following:

  • argparse for command line arguments
  • inspect.lua for dumping lua objects
  • toml.lua for dealing with config file

docs.title: C++ binding
docs.text: Stormweaver includes several classes/functions implemented in C++, which are usable in lua scripts. The complete list of these functions is available in the Lua C++ reference.

docs.title: Scenario configuration
docs.text:
The argparse library is included, and the C++ runner forwards all arguments to the script, including the scenario filename.

The lua helpers already create a default parser using the global variable argparser, which includes handling the common arguments, so scenarios only have to set up additional scenario specific arguments.

docs.title: Multi-threaded structure
docs.text:
StormWeaver is a multithreaded, concurrent test framework, while lua is a single threaded scripting language. To make the two work together, it uses multiple lua instances:

  • There's one "main" instance used for the scenario startup and setup
  • There's one instance per worker thread when running workloads
  • And any of the above threads also might start background workers, resulting in additional lua threads

The main thread and background workers are lua-first: there's a lua function (main in case of the main thread, user specified for the other) that gets executed, and when this function ends, the thread also exists.

The worker threads are hovever C++-first: the random workload is generated by a C++ runner, and it only executes lua functions when it is neccessary.

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
To ensure that all threads have access to the same functions, the scenario file is loaded for all lua states.

This results in the following limitations:

  • the scenario script, and included other scripts get processed multiple times
  • anything that is executed directly by the script will be executed by all threads
  • while global variables will be available in all threads, their values will be different and won't be synced

In line with this, it is recommended that script/scenario files:

  • shouldn't rely on mutable global variables
  • shouldn't execute code directly in the source file, instead everything should go into the main functions

docs.title: Minimal scenario
docs.text:
require("common")

function setup_tables(worker)
    worker:create_random_tables(5)
end

function main(argv)
    args = argparser:parse(argv)
    conffile = parse_config(args)
    pgconfig = PgConf.new(conffile["default"])

    pgm = PgManager.new(pgconfig)
    pgm:setupAndStartPrimary()
    pgm.primaryNode:init(setup_tables)

    pg1 = pgm:get(1)

    t1 = pgm.primaryNode:initRandomWorkload({ run_seconds = 20, worker_count = 5 })
    t1:run()
    t1:wait_completion()
end

docs.title: Custom arguments
docs.text:
A complete documentation for argparse is available at https://argparse.readthedocs.io/

-- could also be in main
argparser:option("-f --foo", "A sample option.", "bar")

function main(argv)
    args = argparser:parse(argv)
    info("Foo: " .. inspect(args["foo"]))
end

docs.title: Simple postgres manager
docs.text:
It is easy and simple to setup (replicated) PostgreSQL using PgManager:

pgconfig = PgConf.new(conffile["default"])

pgm = PgManager.new(pgconfig)
pgm:setupAndStartPrimary()
pgm:setupAndStartAReplica()
-- can start additional replicas

primary = pgm:get(1)
replica = pgm:get(2)
-- ...

docs.title: Background threads
docs.text:
The following example shows the background thread functionality:

-- global variables shouldn't be used: the values can be different in every thread
var = "foo";

-- functions defined in lua can be called in any thread
function dep()
    info("From dep(): var=" .. var)
end

function bg_thread()
    info("From bgt()")
    dep()

    for i=1,10 do
        -- the receive, receiveIfAny and send functions are special to background threads, 
        -- and used to communication with the creator thread
        msg = receive();

        info("Received message: " .. msg);

        if msg == "exit" then
            break;
        end;
    end
end

function main(argv)

    info("Starting background thread...")

    -- won't be reflected in the thread, do not depend on global state
    var = "bar"

    task = BackgroundThread.run("bglog", "bg_thread")

    info("While background thread running ... ")

    task:send("hey!")
    task:send("foo!")
    task:send("bar!")

    info("While background thread still running ... ")

    task:send("exit")

    task:join()

    info("All done")

end
